subject: Chemistry
q: The volume of gas A is twice than that of gas B. The compressibility factor of gas A is thrice than that of gas B at same temperature. What are the pressures of the gases for equal number of moles?
question: The volume of gas A is twice than that of gas B. The compressibility factor of gas A is thrice than that of gas B at same temperature. What are the pressures of the gases for equal number of moles?
solution:
PV=ZnRT

VA=2VB and ZA=3ZB

P=ZnRTV

PAPB=ZAVBZBVA=3ZB×VBZB×2VB=32

2PA=3PB

At constant T and moles; P∝ZV

subject: Chemistry
q:
Consider the van der Waal's constants, a and b, for the following gases.
Gas Ar Ne Kr Xe
a/(atm dm6 mol-2) 1.3 0.2 5.1 4.1
b/(10-2 dm3 mol-1) 3.2 1.7 1.0 5.0

Which gas is expected to have the highest critical temperature?
question:
Consider the van der Waal's constants, a and b, for the following gases.
Gas Ar Ne Kr Xe
a/(atm dm6 mol-2) 1.3 0.2 5.1 4.1
b/(10-2 dm3 mol-1) 3.2 1.7 1.0 5.0

Which gas is expected to have the highest critical temperature?
solution:
Critical temperature is :-

TC=8a27Rb
for Kr the value of ab is the highest so, Tc for Kr will be highest.
ab for Ar=1.33.2=0.406
ab for Ne=0.21.7=0.118
ab for Kr=5.11=5.1
ab for Xe=4.15=0.82

subject: Chemistry
q: The relationship among most probable velocity, average velocity and root mean square velocity is respectively
question: The relationship among most probable velocity, average velocity and root mean square velocity is respectively
solution: Most probable speed $\left(C^*\right)=\sqrt{\frac{2 R T}{M}}$ Average speed $(\bar{C})=\sqrt{\frac{8 R T}{\pi M}}$ Root mean square velocity $(C)=\sqrt{\frac{3 R T}{M}}$ $C^*: \bar{C}: C=\sqrt{2}: \sqrt{\frac{8}{\pi}}: \sqrt{3}$

PV=nRTV=nRTPVH2=VA (Given)

subject: Chemistry
q: According to the kinetic theory of gases, in an ideal gas, between two successive collisions a gas molecule travels
question: According to the kinetic theory of gases, in an ideal gas, between two successive collisions a gas molecule travels
solution: According to kinetic theory the gas molecules travel in a straight line path but show haphazard motion due to collisions.

subject: Chemistry
q: Benzene and toluene form nearly ideal solutions. At $20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, the vapour pressure of benzene is 75 torr and that of toluene is 22 torr. The partial vapour pressure of benzene at $20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ for a solution containing $78 \mathrm{~g}$ of benzene and $46 \mathrm{~g}$ of toluene in torr is
question: Benzene and toluene form nearly ideal solutions. At $20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, the vapour pressure of benzene is 75 torr and that of toluene is 22 torr. The partial vapour pressure of benzene at $20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ for a solution containing $78 \mathrm{~g}$ of benzene and $46 \mathrm{~g}$ of toluene in torr is
solution: $\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{B}}=\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{B}}^{\circ} \times \mathrm{B}=75 \times \frac{1}{1.5}=50$ torr

subject: Chemistry
q:
Van der Waal's equation for a gas is stated as,

P=nRTV-nb-anV2.

This equation reduces to the perfect gas equation, P=nRTV When,
question:
Van der Waal's equation for a gas is stated as,

P=nRTV-nb-anV2.

This equation reduces to the perfect gas equation, P=nRTV When,
options: temperature is sufficiently high and pressure is low.
both temperature and pressure are very low.
both temperature and pressure are very high.
temperature is sufficiently low and pressure is high.
solution:
At very high temperatures & low pressures, the characteristic constant behaviour can be understood from virial expansion

Z = PV m RT = 1 + b - a RT 1 V m + b 2 V m 2 + ....

Vm, molar volume large i.e  b 2 V m 2 → o

i.e Third term in virial expansion can be ignored.

In qualitative terms, the real gas will show ideal behaviour when the intermolecular forces of attraction will be negligible which is possible at high temperature and low pressure.
Also  b - a RT → o

i.e.  Z ≈ 1  and gas behaves ideally.

subject: Chemistry
q:
Which one of the following graphs is not correct for ideal gas?

d= Density, P= Pressure, T=Temperature
question:
Which one of the following graphs is not correct for ideal gas?

d= Density, P= Pressure, T=Temperature
solution:
For ideal gas

PM = dRT

d=PMR1T

Graph between density versus 1/T should be straight line.

Graph between density versus P should be straight line.

So graph between d Vs T is not straight line.

Hence option II is wrong.

question: The intermolecular interaction that is dependent on the inverse cube of distance between the molecules is:
solution:

F∝1r3

r is distance between two dipoles

subject: Chemistry
q: By how many folds the temperature of a gas would increase when the root mean square velocity of the gas molecules in a container of fixed volume is increased from $5 \times 10^4 \mathrm{~cm} / \mathrm{s}$ to $10 \times 10^4 \mathrm{~cm} / \mathrm{s}$ ?
question: By how many folds the temperature of a gas would increase when the root mean square velocity of the gas molecules in a container of fixed volume is increased from $5 \times 10^4 \mathrm{~cm} / \mathrm{s}$ to $10 \times 10^4 \mathrm{~cm} / \mathrm{s}$ ?
solution:
r.m.s. velocity $\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{rms}}=\sqrt{\frac{3 R T}{M}}$
i.e., $\frac{\mathrm{V}_1}{\mathrm{~V}_2}=\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{T}_1}{\mathrm{~T}_2}}$
$\frac{5 \times 10^4}{10 \times 10^4}=\frac{1}{2}=\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{T}_1}{\mathrm{~T}_2}}$
$\therefore \mathrm{T}_2=4 \mathrm{T}_1$

subject: Chemistry
q:
An empty LPG cylinder weighs 14.8 kg. When full, it weighs 29.0 kg and shows a pressure of 3.47 atm. In the course of use at ambient temperature, the mass of the cylinder is reduced to 23.0 kg. The final pressure inside the cylinder is _____ atm. (Nearest integer)

(Assume LPG to be an ideal gas)
question:
An empty LPG cylinder weighs 14.8 kg. When full, it weighs 29.0 kg and shows a pressure of 3.47 atm. In the course of use at ambient temperature, the mass of the cylinder is reduced to 23.0 kg. The final pressure inside the cylinder is _____ atm. (Nearest integer)

(Assume LPG to be an ideal gas)
solution:
Initial mass of gas =29-14.8=14.2Kg

mass of gas used : =29-23=6Kg

gas left =14.2-6=8.2Kg

(1) 3.47×V=14.2×103M×R×T

(2) p×V=8.2×103M×R×T

Divide : (1)/(2)⇒  3.47P=14.28.2

P=2.003

solution:
u=3RT28

u′=3R×2T14

uu′=14

uu′=12

u′=2u

(Round off to the Nearest Integer). [Assume chlorine is an ideal gas at STP <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>083</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi><mo> </mo><mi>bar</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>mol</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math><math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>,</mo><msub><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>023</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>23</mn></msup><mo>]</mo></math>
question: The number of chlorine atoms in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mL</mi></math> of chlorine gas at STP is _______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>21</mn></msup></math>. (Round off to the Nearest Integer). [Assume chlorine is an ideal gas at STP <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>083</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi><mo> </mo><mi>bar</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>mol</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math><math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>,</mo><msub><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>023</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>23</mn></msup><mo>]</mo></math>
solution:
PV=nRT

1.0×201000=N6.023×1023×0.083×273

∴ Number of Cl2 molecules, N=5.3×1020

Hence, Number of Cl -atoms =1.06×1021

≈1×1021

Their equation of state is given as <math><mi mathvariant="normal">p</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">b</mi></mrow></mfrac></math> at <math><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi><mo>.</mo></math> Here, <math><mi mathvariant="normal">b</mi></math> is the van der Waals constant. Which gas will exhibit steepest increase in the plot of <math><mi mathvariant="normal">Z</mi></math> (compression factor) vs <math><mi mathvariant="normal">p</mi><mo>?</mo></math>

Their equation of state is given as <math><mi mathvariant="normal">p</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">b</mi></mrow></mfrac></math> at <math><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi><mo>.</mo></math> Here, <math><mi mathvariant="normal">b</mi></math> is the van der Waals constant. Which gas will exhibit steepest increase in the plot of <math><mi mathvariant="normal">Z</mi></math> (compression factor) vs <math><mi mathvariant="normal">p</mi><mo>?</mo></math>
solution: As the b increases the slope increases. We know that b represents the effective size of the molecules. Since, Xe has the biggest size among given noble gases because on moving down the group size increases due to increase in shell, hence Xe will have the highest slope.

The molar mass of the gas in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>gmol</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> is : (Given : <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>082</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>atmK</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><msup><mi>mol</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
solution:
Weight of gas =40.5-40=0.5 g

Mass of liquid =135.5-40=95 g.

Volume of glass vessel =95.0 g0.95 g/ml

=100ml.

∴ PV=WMRT

0.82 atm100×10-3=0.5M0.082250

M=125 g/mol

subject: Chemistry
q:
The volume occupied by 4.75 g of acetylene gas at 50°C and 740 mm Hg pressure is ____________ L. (Rounded off to the nearest integer)

[Given R=0.0826 L atm K-1 mol-1]
question:
The volume occupied by 4.75 g of acetylene gas at 50°C and 740 mm Hg pressure is ____________ L. (Rounded off to the nearest integer)

[Given R=0.0826 L atm K-1 mol-1]
solution:
Given mass=4.75 g⇒C2H2g

⇒Moles=4.7526 mol

Temperature=50+273=323 K

P=740760atm

R=0.0826ℓ atmmol K

⇒V=nRTP=4.7526×0.0826×323740760

⇒V=96314.07819240=5.0059ℓ≃5ℓ

subject: Chemistry
q: Which of the following is not an assumption of the kinetic theory of gases?
question: Which of the following is not an assumption of the kinetic theory of gases?
options: Collisions of gas particles are perfectly elastic.
A gas consists of many identical particles which are in continual motion.
At high pressure, gas particles are difficult to compress.
solution: At high pressures gas particles are difficult to compress rather they are not compressible at all.

[nearest integer]
question: A rigid nitrogen tank stored inside a laboratory has a pressure of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn></math> atm at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>06</mn><mo>:</mo><mn>00</mn></math> am when the temperature is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>27</mn><mo> </mo><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>. At <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>03</mn><mo>:</mo><mn>00</mn><mi>pm</mi></math>, when the temperature is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>45</mn><mo> </mo><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>, the pressure in the tank will be _____ atm. [nearest integer]
solution:
According to Gay Lussac law At constant volume and mole

P1T1=P2T2

30300=P2318

P2=30300×318=31.8≈32

subject: Chemistry
q: Which one of the following statements is NOT true about the effect of an increase in temperature on the distribution of molecular speeds in a gas?
question: Which one of the following statements is NOT true about the effect of an increase in temperature on the distribution of molecular speeds in a gas?
options: The fraction of the molecules with the most probable speed increases
The area under the distribution curve remains the same as under the lower temperature
solution: Most probable velocity increase and fraction of molecule possessing most probable velocity decreases

subject: Chemistry
q:
At the sea level, the dry air mass percentage composition is given as nitrogen gas : 70.0, oxygen gas : 27.0 and argon gas : 3.0. If total pressure is 1.15 atm , then calculate the ratio of followings respectively :
(i) partial pressure of nitrogen gas to partial pressure of oxygen gas
(ii) partial pressure of oxygen gas to partial pressure of argon gas
(Given : Molar mass of $\mathrm{N}, \mathrm{O}$ and Ar are 14, 16, and $40 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ respectively)
question:
At the sea level, the dry air mass percentage composition is given as nitrogen gas : 70.0, oxygen gas : 27.0 and argon gas : 3.0. If total pressure is 1.15 atm , then calculate the ratio of followings respectively :
(i) partial pressure of nitrogen gas to partial pressure of oxygen gas
(ii) partial pressure of oxygen gas to partial pressure of argon gas
(Given : Molar mass of $\mathrm{N}, \mathrm{O}$ and Ar are 14, 16, and $40 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ respectively)
solution:
$\frac{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{N}_2}}{\mathrm{Po}_2}=\frac{\mathrm{x}_{\mathrm{N}_2} \cdot \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{T}}}{\mathrm{x}_{\mathrm{O}_2} \cdot \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{T}}}=\frac{\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{N}_2}}{\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{O}_2}}$ {using Dalton's law of partial pressure}
$\begin{aligned} & =\frac{70 / 28}{27 / 32}=2.96 \\ & \frac{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{O}_2}}{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{Ar}}}=\frac{\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{O}_2}}{\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{Ar}}}=\frac{27 / 32}{3 / 40}=11.25\end{aligned}$

subject: Chemistry
q: For a reaction, $\mathrm{N}_2 \mathrm{O}_{5(\mathrm{~g})} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{NO}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}$ in a constant volume container, no products were present initially. The final pressure of the system when $50 \%$ of reaction gets completed is
question: For a reaction, $\mathrm{N}_2 \mathrm{O}_{5(\mathrm{~g})} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{NO}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}$ in a constant volume container, no products were present initially. The final pressure of the system when $50 \%$ of reaction gets completed is
solution: $\begin{gathered}x=\frac{P_0}{2} \\ P_{\text {total }}=P_0-\frac{P_0}{2}+P_0+\frac{P_0}{4}=\frac{7}{4} P_0\end{gathered}$

subject: Chemistry
q: Three bulbs are filled with CH4, CO2 and Ne as shown in the picture. The bulbs are connected through pipes of zero volume. When the stopcocks are opened and the temperature is kept constant throughout, the pressure of the system is found to be______ atm. (Nearest integer).
question: Three bulbs are filled with CH4, CO2 and Ne as shown in the picture. The bulbs are connected through pipes of zero volume. When the stopcocks are opened and the temperature is kept constant throughout, the pressure of the system is found to be______ atm. (Nearest integer).
solution:
The total number of moles remains constant.

n1+n2+n3=nf

n1,n2, n3 moles of gases in the individual containers. nf is resultant moles.

Using ideal gas equation PV=nRT, we van write as 

P1V1RT+P2V2RT+P3V3RT=PfVfRT

P1V1+P2V2+P3V3=PfVf

2×2+4×3+3×4=Pf×9

Pf=289≈3

subject: Chemistry
q: An open vessel at <math><msup><mrow><mn>27</mn></mrow><mrow><mi>o</mi></mrow></msup><mi>C</mi></math> is heated until two fifth of the air (assumed as an ideal gas) in it has escaped from the vessel. Assuming that the volume of the vessel remains constant, the temperature to which the vessel has heated is:
question: An open vessel at <math><msup><mrow><mn>27</mn></mrow><mrow><mi>o</mi></mrow></msup><mi>C</mi></math> is heated until two fifth of the air (assumed as an ideal gas) in it has escaped from the vessel. Assuming that the volume of the vessel remains constant, the temperature to which the vessel has heated is:
solution:
n1T1=n2T2

⇒n×300=n-2n5T2

⇒300=35T2

⇒T2=500 K

subject: Chemistry
q: At very high pressures, the compressibility factor of one mole of a gas is given by:
question: At very high pressures, the compressibility factor of one mole of a gas is given by:
solution:
P+aV2V-b=RT

at very high pressure P≫aV2

PV-b=RT

PV-Pb=RT

∴Z=1+PbRT compressibility factor

subject: Chemistry
q: Assuming ideal gas behavior, the ratio of density of ammonia to that of hydrogen chloride at same temperature and pressure is: (Atomic weight of Cl is 35.5 u)
question: Assuming ideal gas behavior, the ratio of density of ammonia to that of hydrogen chloride at same temperature and pressure is: (Atomic weight of Cl is 35.5 u)
solution:
d=P(M.W.)RT

dNH3dHCl= (M.W.)NH3(M.W.)HCl= 1736.5=0.46

subject: Chemistry
q: Which one of the following is the wrong assumption of kinetic theory of gases ?
question: Which one of the following is the wrong assumption of kinetic theory of gases ?
options: Momentum and energy always remain conserved.
Pressure is the result of elastic collision of molecules with the container's wall.
Molecules are separated by great distances compared to their sizes.
All the molecules move in straight line between collision and with same velocity.
solution: Molecules move very fast in all directions in a straight line by colliding with each other but with different velocity.
